---
title: "Document Classification"
author: "Desiree Thomas, Kiera Griffiths"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Approach

The task for this assignment is to create a classification system to identify whether an email that was received is spam or "ham". The approach for this project will be to import the SpamAssassin corpus into our environment; we will attempt reproducibility during this section. A function will be used to iterate through the directories as it reads each text file and then we will store it in a dataframe. We will include a cleaning phase to ensure that the model focuses on the content of the email, rather than the formatting or other noise; one of the main points of this is to reduce the risk of overfitting. For this to work and allow the model to "read" through the emails, we cannot use any data format or file type; vectorization will need to occur as the model will need to have the now cleaned text as a Document-Term Matrix. The model we will use for this assignment has priliminarily been assigned as Naive Bayes; though depending on the baseline we may experiment with a Support Vector Machine (as suggested by Gemini) or even Random Forest. Lastly, we will run an evaluation to determine where our model is either failing or experiencing bottlenecks; for this we will determine both the models precision and recall. Potential pitfalls include an imbalanced class which is sometimes caused by a model "guessing" ham frequently - this may lead to an inflated accuracy score. We may also encounter encoding errors (though we may not encounter this in the specific corpus that we are using). To extend this further, we may try this against web-pages that were scraped and tagged, to compare classification against the web pages and emails.
